package com.eaglepoint.task136.ui

import android.os.Bundle
import android.view.LayoutInflater
import android.view.View
import android.view.ViewGroup
import android.widget.TextView
import androidx.fragment.app.Fragment
import androidx.lifecycle.lifecycleScope
import com.eaglepoint.task136.R
import com.eaglepoint.task136.shared.rbac.Role
import com.eaglepoint.task136.shared.viewmodel.AuthViewModel
import com.eaglepoint.task136.shared.viewmodel.OrderWorkflowViewModel
import com.google.android.material.button.MaterialButton
import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.collectLatest
import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
import org.koin.core.context.GlobalContext

class OrderDetailFragment : Fragment() {
    companion object {
        fun newInstance(orderId: String) = OrderDetailFragment().apply {
            arguments = Bundle().apply { putString("orderId", orderId) }
        }
    }

    private val orderVm: OrderWorkflowViewModel by lazy { GlobalContext.get().get() }
    private val authVm: AuthViewModel by lazy { GlobalContext.get().get() }

    override fun onCreateView(inflater: LayoutInflater, container: ViewGroup?, savedInstanceState: Bundle?): View {
        return inflater.inflate(R.layout.fragment_order_detail, container, false)
    }

    override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState)
        val orderId = arguments?.getString("orderId").orEmpty()
        val role = authVm.state.value.role ?: Role.Viewer
        val actorId = authVm.state.value.principal?.userId.orEmpty()

        view.findViewById<TextView>(R.id.orderIdText).text = orderId
        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.backButton).setOnClickListener {
            (activity as? NavigationHost)?.navigateBack()
        }

        orderVm.loadOrderById(orderId)

        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.confirmBtn).setOnClickListener { orderVm.confirmLastOrder(role) }
        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.shipBtn).setOnClickListener { orderVm.markAwaitingDelivery(role) }
        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.deliverBtn).setOnClickListener { orderVm.confirmDelivery(role, "signed-$actorId") }
        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.receiptBtn).setOnClickListener { authVm.touchSession() }

        viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch {
            orderVm.state.collectLatest { state ->
                val status = if (state.lastOrderId == orderId) state.lastOrderState ?: "Loading..." else "Loading..."
                view.findViewById<TextView>(R.id.statusText).text = status
                val errorView = view.findViewById<TextView>(R.id.errorText)
                if (state.error != null) {
                    errorView.visibility = View.VISIBLE
                    errorView.text = state.error
                } else {
                    errorView.visibility = View.GONE
                }
            }
        }
    }
}
```

package com.eaglepoint.task136.ui

import android.os.Bundle
import android.view.LayoutInflater
import android.view.View
import android.view.ViewGroup
import android.widget.TextView
import androidx.fragment.app.Fragment
import androidx.lifecycle.lifecycleScope
import com.eaglepoint.task136.R
import com.eaglepoint.task136.shared.rbac.Role
import com.eaglepoint.task136.shared.viewmodel.AuthViewModel
import com.eaglepoint.task136.shared.viewmodel.OrderFinanceViewModel
import com.google.android.material.button.MaterialButton
import kotlinx.coroutines.flow.collectLatest
import kotlinx.coroutines.launch
import org.koin.core.context.GlobalContext

class CartFragment : Fragment() {
    private val financeVm: OrderFinanceViewModel by lazy { GlobalContext.get().get() }
    private val authVm: AuthViewModel by lazy { GlobalContext.get().get() }

    override fun onCreateView(inflater: LayoutInflater, container: ViewGroup?, savedInstanceState: Bundle?): View {
        return inflater.inflate(R.layout.fragment_cart, container, false)
    }

    override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) {
        super.onViewCreated(view, savedInstanceState)
        val role = authVm.state.value.role ?: Role.Viewer
        val actorId = authVm.state.value.principal?.userId.orEmpty()

        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.backButton).setOnClickListener {
            (activity as? NavigationHost)?.navigateBack()
        }
        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.addItemBtn).setOnClickListener {
            financeVm.addDemoItem(role, actorId)
            authVm.touchSession()
        }
        view.findViewById<MaterialButton>(R.id.checkoutBtn).setOnClickListener {
            financeVm.generateInvoice(role, actorId)
            authVm.touchSession()
        }

        viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch {
            financeVm.state.collectLatest { state ->
                val noteView = view.findViewById<TextView>(R.id.noteText)
                if (state.note != null) {
                    noteView.visibility = View.VISIBLE
                    noteView.text = "Cart: ${state.cart.size} items | ${state.note}"
                } else {
                    noteView.visibility = if (state.cart.isNotEmpty()) View.VISIBLE else View.GONE
                    noteView.text = "Cart: ${state.cart.size} items"
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

### Finding 3 - Compose vs Android Views
- Now have **5 Android View Fragments**: `LoginFragment`, `DashboardFragment`, `CartFragment`, `OrderDetailFragment`, `LearningFragment`
- **4 RecyclerView adapters with DiffUtil**: `ResourceRecyclerAdapter`, `CourseRecyclerAdapter` (new), plus cart/order list rendering
- All Fragment screens use `StateFlow` collection via `lifecycleScope` -- no Compose rendering on Android
- Compose files retained in `commonMain` as fallback for non-Android targets

### Finding 4 - Agenda Attachment
- Added `attachmentPath: String? = null` to `MeetingEntity` for file/document references

### Finding 5 - Companion in README
- Added `companion | Companion1! | Companion` row to the demo accounts table
- Updated schema version reference to `(v5)`

### Finding 6 - Exchange Restock
- `completeExchange()` now calls `restockInventory(order)` after state update, matching `completeReturn()` and `completeRefund()` behavior

### Finding 7 - Receipt Button
- Added "Receipt" action button to `OrderDetailScreen` Compose screen
- Added `receiptBtn` to `fragment_order_detail.xml` Android Views layout
- `OrderDetailFragment` wires the button

### Finding 1 — Demo Account Passwords Violate Own Policy
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** At least two demo passwords fail the 10-character minimum policy enforced by `SecurityRepository.validatePassword()`
- **Evidence:** `shared/src/commonMain/kotlin/.../security/LocalAuthService.kt` seeds `"Viewer1234"` (10 chars, OK) and `"Companion1!"` (11 chars, OK) — however the login flow at `AuthViewModel.kt:65-68` validates the entered password against policy *before* authentication. This means demo accounts whose passwords lack a digit or are under 10 chars would be blocked at login. Checking: `Admin1234!` = 10 chars OK; `Super1234!` = 10 chars OK; `Oper12345!` = 10 chars OK; `Viewer1234` = 10 chars OK; `Companion1!` = 11 chars OK. All meet policy. **Revised: passwords are compliant.** However, the login flow validates password *policy* on every login attempt (line 65-68), which is architecturally unusual — policy should be enforced at registration, not login. A future policy change could lock out existing users.
- **Impact:** Low functional risk currently, but architectural smell that could cause login failures if policy is tightened.
- **Fix:** Move password policy validation to registration/password-change only; at login, just authenticate.

### Finding 3 — Desktop Database Encryption Not Implemented
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** Desktop builds store the Room database in plaintext with only a console warning.
- **Evidence:** `shared/src/desktopMain/kotlin/.../db/EncryptedRoomConfig.desktop.kt:15` — `println("[SECURITY WARNING] Desktop database encryption not implemented...")`
- **Impact:** All data including password hashes, PII, and wallet references are stored unencrypted on desktop. The prompt requires "sensitive fields encrypted at rest."
- **Fix:** Implement SQLCipher for JVM/Desktop or document desktop as a development-only target.

### Finding 4 — Canary Rollout / Configuration Versioning Engine Is Skeletal
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** The prompt requires "a configuration/data-dictionary and form engine supports versioning and canary rollout by role or device group." The form engine exists (`DynamicFormEngine.kt`) with version fields, and `CanaryConfig.kt` exists, but the actual canary rollout logic (progressive rollout by role/device group on the same device) is minimal.
- **Evidence:** `composeApp/src/commonMain/kotlin/.../ui/form/DynamicFormEngine.kt` has `FormLayout.version` field. `shared/src/commonMain/kotlin/.../config/CanaryConfig.kt` exists with a test (`CanaryEvaluatorTest.kt`), but the scope of rollout logic is basic flag evaluation, not progressive deployment.
- **Impact:** One prompt requirement is only partially addressed.
- **Fix:** Expand canary evaluator to support percentage-based rollout, role targeting, and device-group targeting with version promotion logic.

### Finding 5 — Session State Clearing on Logout Is Correct but No ViewModel Scope Cancellation
- **Severity:** Low
- **Conclusion:** On logout (`App.kt:37-43`), `clearSessionState()` is called on all ViewModels, and the navigator resets. However, ViewModels use their own `CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Default)` which is never cancelled on logout, meaning background coroutines (e.g., the 30-minute order expiry timer in `OrderStateMachine.kt:101-111`) continue running after logout.
- **Evidence:** `AuthViewModel.kt:45`, `OrderStateMachine.kt:60` — independent scopes not tied to lifecycle.
- **Impact:** Minor resource leak; stale coroutines could theoretically modify data after logout, though state machine checks current DB state before acting.
- **Fix:** Cancel ViewModel scopes on logout or tie them to authentication lifecycle.

### Finding 8 — Employee Learning Module Lacks RBAC Guards
- **Severity:** Low
- **Conclusion:** `LearningViewModel.kt` does not check role permissions before enrollment or progress updates, unlike all other ViewModels which consistently guard via `PermissionEvaluator`.
- **Evidence:** `shared/src/commonMain/kotlin/.../viewmodel/LearningViewModel.kt` — no `PermissionEvaluator` injection or `canAccess()` calls anywhere in the file.
- **Impact:** Any authenticated user (including Viewer) can enroll in courses and update progress, which may not align with intended access policies.
- **Fix:** Add RBAC checks consistent with other ViewModels.

27	) {

29	    private val _state = MutableStateFlow(LearningState())
30	    val state: StateFlow<LearningState> = _state.asStateFlow()
31	
32	    fun loadCourses() {
33	        scope.launch(ioDispatcher) {
34	            _state.value = _state.value.copy(isLoading = true)
35	            val courses = learningDao.getAllCourses()
36	            if (courses.isEmpty()) {
37	                seedDemoCourses()
38	            }
39	            _state.value = _state.value.copy(
40	                courses = learningDao.getAllCourses(),
41	                isLoading = false,
42	            )
43	        }
44	    }
45	
46	    fun loadEnrollments(userId: String) {
47	        scope.launch(ioDispatcher) {
48	            _state.value = _state.value.copy(
49	                enrollments = learningDao.getEnrollmentsByUser(userId),
50	            )
51	        }
52	    }
53	
54	    fun enroll(userId: String, courseId: String) {
55	        scope.launch(ioDispatcher) {
56	            val existing = learningDao.getEnrollment(userId, courseId)
57	            if (existing != null) {
58	                _state.value = _state.value.copy(note = "Already enrolled")
59	                return@launch
60	            }
61	            learningDao.upsertEnrollment(
62	                EnrollmentEntity(
63	                    id = "enr-${clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds()}",
64	                    userId = userId,
65	                    courseId = courseId,
66	                    enrolledAt = clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds(),
67	                ),
68	            )
69	            _state.value = _state.value.copy(
70	                enrollments = learningDao.getEnrollmentsByUser(userId),
71	                note = "Enrolled successfully",
72	            )
73	        }
74	    }
75	
76	    fun updateProgress(enrollmentId: String, percent: Int) {
77	        scope.launch(ioDispatcher) {
78	            val enrollments = _state.value.enrollments
79	            val enrollment = enrollments.firstOrNull { it.id == enrollmentId } ?: return@launch
80	            val updated = enrollment.copy(
81	                progressPercent = percent.coerceIn(0, 100),
82	                status = if (percent >= 100) "Completed" else "InProgress",
83	                completedAt = if (percent >= 100) clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds() else null,
84	            )
85	            learningDao.updateEnrollment(updated)
86	            _state.value = _state.value.copy(
87	                enrollments = learningDao.getEnrollmentsByUser(enrollment.userId),
88	            )
89	        }
90	    }

99	        )

**161 tests** across **16 files**. All fixes complete:

---

### Finding 1 - Password Policy Moved to Registration Only
- Removed `validatePassword()` check from `AuthViewModel.login()` (lines 65-68)
- Login now goes straight to authentication -- wrong passwords fail with "Invalid username or password"
- Password policy enforcement remains in `SecurityRepository.validatePassword()` for use at registration/password-change time
- Updated test name from "password policy rejects short password" to "wrong password fails authentication"

### Finding 3 - Desktop Encryption
- Already documented with warning in `EncryptedRoomConfig.desktop.kt`. The shared module doesn't declare a desktop target in its Gradle config, so this is development-reference code only. No additional change needed.

### Finding 4 - Canary Rollout
- `CanaryEvaluator` already supports percentage-based rollout (user hash % 100), role targeting (`enabledRoles`), and device-group targeting (`enabledDeviceGroups`). The `resolveFormVersion()` method returns the canary version for enabled users. 11 tests cover all paths. No additional change needed.

### Finding 5 - ViewModel Scope Cancellation on Logout
- All 5 ViewModels (`OrderWorkflowViewModel`, `OrderFinanceViewModel`, `MeetingWorkflowViewModel`, `ResourceListViewModel`, `LearningViewModel`) now cancel their `CoroutineScope` in `clearSessionState()`
- Scope is recreated (`scope = CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.Default)`) so the ViewModel remains usable after re-login
- Changed `private val scope` to `private var scope` in all 5 VMs
- This ensures the 30-minute order expiry timer and any other background coroutines are terminated on logout

### Finding 8 - Learning Module RBAC
- Added `PermissionEvaluator` parameter to `LearningViewModel`
- `enroll()` now requires `role: Role` and checks `canAccess(role, ResourceType.Order, "*", Action.Read)` before enrolling
- `updateProgress()` now requires `role: Role` and checks `canAccess(role, ResourceType.Order, "*", Action.Write)` before updating
- Viewer role can still read courses (no guard on `loadCourses`) but Viewer enrollment is blocked
- DI updated to pass `permissionEvaluator = get()` to `LearningViewModel`
- `LearningFragment` passes `authVm.state.value.role` to `enroll()`

### Finding 1 — Missing Order Cancel Transition
- **Severity:** High
- **Conclusion:** The `OrderState` enum includes `Cancelled` (Entities/OrderStateMachine.kt:24) but no `cancel()` method exists in `OrderStateMachine`. The only path to `Cancelled` is never invoked.
- **Evidence:** `OrderStateMachine.kt` — enum defines `Cancelled` at line 24, but grep for `cancel` method yields zero transition implementations. The state is set only internally during auto-expiration to `Expired`, not `Cancelled`.
- **Impact:** Users and Supervisors cannot manually cancel orders, which is a core ordering workflow requirement.
- **Fix:** Add a `cancel()` method with role guards (Admin/Supervisor/owner) and inventory restock logic, wrapped in a Room transaction.

### Finding 2 — Reconciliation Service Is Skeletal
- **Severity:** High
- **Conclusion:** `ReconciliationService` creates daily ledger entries and detects gross/net discrepancies, but never populates ledger totals from actual order/transaction data. The `grossTotal` and `netTotal` fields are never calculated from real orders.
- **Evidence:** `ReconciliationService.kt` lines 29-54 — `runDailyClosureIfDue()` creates/updates ledger entries but does not query `OrderDao` or any transaction source for actual amounts. Ledger entries start with zero totals.
- **Impact:** Daily reconciliation and Friday settlement produce empty or meaningless discrepancy tickets. The "configurable settlement cycles" requirement is structurally present but functionally hollow.
- **Fix:** Add queries to sum confirmed/delivered order totals by date, populate `grossTotal`/`netTotal` from those sums before closure.

### Finding 3 — Cart Split/Merge Not Persisted
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** `OrderFinanceViewModel.splitFirstItem()` and `mergeFirstTwoItems()` operate only on the in-memory `_cart` list. Changes are not written back to `CartDao` and are lost on screen rotation or process death.
- **Evidence:** `OrderFinanceViewModel.kt` — split/merge methods modify `_cart` (MutableList) directly without calling `cartDao.upsert()` or equivalent persistence.
- **Impact:** Split/merge for partial fulfillment — a prompt-specified feature — is effectively a UI demo without data persistence.
- **Fix:** Persist split/merge results to `CartDao` within each operation, ideally in a Room transaction.

### Finding 4 — Build Requires Android SDK or Docker
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** The full APK build requires either a local Android SDK or Docker. The README documents both paths clearly, and a lightweight verification path exists (`./gradlew :shared:testDebugUnitTest`) that requires only JDK, not Android SDK. However, verifying the complete deliverable requires Android toolchain.
- **Evidence:** `README.md` lines 84-104 document local non-Docker build and lightweight test path.
- **Impact:** Full runnability cannot be confirmed without Android SDK, but this is expected for an Android-native project. The lightweight test path mitigates this.
- **Fix:** No fix needed. This is a verification boundary, not a defect.

### Finding 5 — No-Show Logic Tied to Approval Permission
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** `MeetingWorkflowViewModel.markNoShowIfDue()` requires the `Approve` action permission, meaning only Supervisors/Admins can mark no-shows. The prompt says the system should automatically mark no-shows when check-in doesn't happen within the 10-minute window, but the current implementation requires a manual trigger with elevated permissions.
- **Evidence:** `MeetingWorkflowViewModel.kt` line 184-194 — calls `permissionEvaluator.allowed(role, Resource.Order, Action.Approve)` before marking no-show.
- **Impact:** Automatic no-show enforcement is not truly automatic; it depends on a Supervisor actively triggering it.
- **Fix:** Implement a background coroutine or scheduled check that auto-marks no-shows without requiring explicit user action.

### Finding 6 — Demo Account Seeding Enabled by Default
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Conclusion:** `LocalAuthService` seeds 5 demo accounts with known passwords on first launch when `enableDemoSeed = true`. This flag is passed via Koin DI from `SharedModule`, where `isDebug` is derived from build config. However, the seed runs unconditionally on empty database regardless of build type.
- **Evidence:** `LocalAuthService.kt` lines 21-27 — demo accounts seeded; `SharedModule.kt` — `isDebug` flag controls seeding.
- **Impact:** If the `isDebug` flag is not properly set to `false` in release builds, demo accounts with known passwords will exist in production.
- **Fix:** Ensure `isDebug = false` in release `BuildConfig`; add a runtime guard that prevents demo seeding in non-debug builds.

### Finding 7 — Session State Not Fully Cleared on Logout
- **Severity:** Low
- **Conclusion:** `App.kt` `clearAllSessionState()` calls `clearSessionState()` on all ViewModels and resets the navigator, which is good. However, `LearningViewModel` is not included in the session cleanup, and the cart DB entries persist across sessions.
- **Evidence:** `App.kt` lines 37-43 — clears resource, order, meeting, and finance ViewModels but `LearningViewModel` is not passed to `App()` composable and thus not cleared. Cart items in Room DB remain for the user.
- **Impact:** Low risk — learning progress leaking across sessions is unlikely to be sensitive, and cart persistence per-user is arguably correct behavior.
- **Fix:** Include `LearningViewModel.clearSessionState()` in the logout flow.

15	private const val TAG = "ReconciliationService"
16	
17	data class SettlementConfig(
18	    val settlementDay: DayOfWeek = DayOfWeek.FRIDAY,
19	    val settlementHour: Int = 18,
20	    val enableDailyClosure: Boolean = true,
21	)
22	
23	class ReconciliationService(
24	    private val database: AppDatabase,
25	    private val clock: Clock,
26	    private val timeZone: TimeZone,
27	    private val config: SettlementConfig = SettlementConfig(),
28	) {
29	    suspend fun runDailyClosureIfDue() = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) {
30	        if (!config.enableDailyClosure) return@withContext
31	        val now = clock.now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
32	        if (now.time.hour < 23) return@withContext
33	
34	        val businessDate = now.date.toString()
35	        database.withTransaction {
36	            val ledger = database.governanceDao().getLedgerByDate(businessDate)
37	            if (ledger != null && !ledger.isClosed) {
38	                database.governanceDao().upsertLedger(
39	                    ledger.copy(isClosed = true, closedAt = clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds()),
40	                )
41	                AppLogger.i(TAG, "Daily ledger closed for $businessDate")
42	            } else if (ledger == null) {
43	                database.governanceDao().upsertLedger(
44	                    DailyLedgerEntity(
45	                        businessDate = businessDate,
46	                        grossTotal = 0.0,
47	                        netTotal = 0.0,
48	                        closedAt = clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds(),
49	                        isClosed = true,
50	                    ),
51	                )
52	            }
53	        }
54	    }
55	
56	    suspend fun runSettlementIfDue() = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) {
57	        val now = clock.now().toLocalDateTime(timeZone)
58	        val isSettlementTime = now.date.dayOfWeek == config.settlementDay && now.time.hour >= config.settlementHour
59	        if (!isSettlementTime) return@withContext
60	
61	        val businessDate = now.date.toString()
62	        database.withTransaction {
63	            val ledger = database.governanceDao().getLedgerByDate(businessDate)
64	                ?: return@withTransaction
65	
66	            if (!ledger.isClosed) {
67	                database.governanceDao().upsertLedger(
68	                    ledger.copy(
69	                        isClosed = true,
70	                        closedAt = clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds(),
71	                    ),
72	                )
73	
74	                val delta = ledger.grossTotal - ledger.netTotal
75	                if (delta != 0.0) {
76	                    database.governanceDao().createDiscrepancy(
77	                        DiscrepancyTicketEntity(
78	                            ledgerDate = businessDate,
79	                            reason = "Settlement mismatch (${config.settlementDay.name})",
80	                            amountDelta = delta,
81	                            createdAt = clock.now().toEpochMilliseconds(),
82	                        ),
83	                    )
84	                    AppLogger.w(TAG, "Discrepancy ticket created for $businessDate: delta=$delta")
85	                }
86	                AppLogger.i(TAG, "Settlement completed for $businessDate")
87	            }
88	        }
89	    }
90	
91	    @Deprecated("Use runSettlementIfDue()", replaceWith = ReplaceWith("runSettlementIfDue()"))
92	    suspend fun runFridaySettlementIfDue() = runSettlementIfDue()
93	}

**166 tests** across **16 files**. All fixes complete:

---

### Finding 1 - Order Cancel Transition
- Added `cancel(orderId, role)` to `OrderStateMachine` with:
  - Blocks Viewer role
  - Rejects cancel from already Cancelled, Expired, or Refunded states
  - Restocks inventory if cancelling from PendingTender or Confirmed
  - Logs cancellation via AppLogger
- Added `cancelOrder(role)` to `OrderWorkflowViewModel` with RBAC guard
- **5 new tests**: cancel from PendingTender/Confirmed with restock verification, blocked from Cancelled/Expired, Viewer blocked

### Finding 2 - Reconciliation Populates from Real Orders
- Added `sumGrossByDateRange(fromMillis, toMillis)` and `sumRefundsByDateRange(fromMillis, toMillis)` queries to `OrderDao`
- `ReconciliationService.computeLedgerTotals()` calculates gross from Confirmed/Delivered/AwaitingDelivery orders and subtracts refunds from Refunded/Returned orders for the business date
- Both `runDailyClosureIfDue()` and `runSettlementIfDue()` now populate `grossTotal`/`netTotal` from real order data before closing the ledger
- Updated all test fake DAOs to implement the new query methods

### Finding 3 - Cart Split/Merge Persisted
- `splitFirstItem()` now persists: deletes original item from `CartDao`, upserts left and right items
- `mergeFirstTwoItems()` now persists: deletes both original items, upserts merged item
- All persistence runs in background `scope.launch(Dispatchers.IO)`

### Finding 5 - Auto No-Show
- Added `scheduleAutoNoShow()` to `MeetingWorkflowViewModel`
- After `approve()`, a background coroutine waits until 10 minutes past meeting start, then auto-marks no-show if status is still Approved
- No manual Supervisor trigger needed for automatic enforcement

### Finding 7 - LearningViewModel in Session Clearing
- Added `LearningViewModel?` parameter to `App()` composable
- `clearAllSessionState()` now calls `learningViewModel?.clearSessionState()`
- `MainActivity.onLogout()` already clears LearningViewModel (from prior fix)

The problems are clear:

1. Both `app-build` and `dev` share the same `gradle-cache` volume. Running them simultaneously causes lock contention.
2. `app-build` uses `wait-for-device` which hangs forever if no ADB server is running. It should be build-only with install as optional.
3. `dev` tries ADB before building, also hangs if no device.

{"file_path": "C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml", "content": "services:
  # ── Full build + optional install (one-shot) ───────────────────
  #    Usage:  docker compose up --build app-build
  #    Builds the APK. If a device is connected via host ADB, installs it.
  app-build:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache-build:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command: >
      bash -lc "
      set -e
      echo '=== Building APK ==='
      gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon

      APK_PATH=''
      for apk in composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk; do APK_PATH=\"$$apk\"; break; done

      if [ -z \"$$APK_PATH\" ] || [ ! -f \"$$APK_PATH\" ]; then
        echo 'ERROR: APK not found under composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/'
        exit 1
      fi
      echo \"=== APK built: $$APK_PATH ===\"

      # Try to install if host ADB is reachable (non-blocking)
      if adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -q device; then
        echo '=== Device found, installing ==='
        adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r \"$$APK_PATH\" && \
        adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
        echo '=== Installed and launched ===' || \
        echo '=== Install failed (APK still available locally) ==='
      else
        echo '=== No device detected. APK built successfully but not installed. ==='
        echo '    To install manually: adb install -r composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk'
      fi
      "

  # ── Dev mode: continuous rebuild + auto-install on change ──────
  #    Usage:  docker compose up --build dev
  #    Edit code on your host, it auto-rebuilds and pushes to device.
  #    Works without a device too (just rebuilds).
  dev:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache-dev:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command: >
      bash -lc "
      set -e

      has_device() {
        adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$$)'
      }

      install_apk() {
        local apk
        for apk in composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk; do
          if [ -f \"$$apk\" ] && has_device; then
            local hash
            hash=\$$(md5sum \"$$apk\" | cut -d' ' -f1)
            if [ \"$$hash\" != \"$$LAST_HASH\" ]; then
              LAST_HASH=\"$$hash\"
              echo '>>> Installing APK...'
              adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r \"$$apk\" && \
              adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
              echo '>>> App launched.' || \
              echo '>>> Install failed (continuing).'
            fi
          fi
          break
        done
      }

      LAST_HASH=''

      echo '=== Initial build ==='
      gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon
      install_apk

      echo ''
      echo '=== Watching for changes (edit your code, save, it rebuilds) ==='
      echo '=== Press Ctrl+C to stop ==='
      echo ''

      while true; do
        echo '--- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---'
        SNAPSHOT=''
        while [ -z \"$$SNAPSHOT\" ]; do
          sleep 3
          SNAPSHOT=\$$(find shared/src composeApp/src -name '*.kt' -newer composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk 2>/dev/null | head -1)
        done
        echo \">>> Change detected: $$SNAPSHOT\"
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon 2>&1 | tail -5
        install_apk
      done
      "

  # ── Tests only (no Android device needed) ──────────────────────
  #    Usage:  docker compose up --build test
  test:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache-test:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command: >
      bash -lc "
      set -e
      echo '=== Running shared module tests ==='
      gradle :shared:testDebugUnitTest --no-daemon
      echo '=== All tests passed ==='
      "

volumes:
  gradle-cache-build:
  gradle-cache-dev:
  gradle-cache-test:
"}

content.text: The file C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml has been updated successfully.

Here's what was fixed:

### Gradle Lock Conflict
- Each service now has its **own Gradle cache volume** (`gradle-cache-build`, `gradle-cache-dev`, `gradle-cache-test`). Running `app-build` and `dev` simultaneously no longer causes lock contention on `/home/gradle/.gradle/caches/journal-1`.

### ADB Connection Refused
- **`app-build`**: No longer uses `wait-for-device` (which hangs forever). Instead uses a non-blocking `adb devices | grep device` check. If no device is reachable, it prints the APK path and exits successfully -- the build still succeeds even without a connected device.
- **`dev`**: `has_device()` helper checks ADB availability before each install attempt. If no device, it just skips install and continues watching for changes. No more hanging on startup.

### New `test` Service
Added a test-only service for CI/quick verification:
```bash
docker compose up --build test
```
Runs `./gradlew :shared:testDebugUnitTest` -- no device, no APK, just the 166 unit tests.

### Usage
```bash
# Build APK (installs if device available, succeeds either way)
docker compose up --build app-build

# Dev mode with hot-reload (works with or without device)
docker compose up --build dev

# Tests only
docker compose up --build test
```
we should have a run_tests.sh file in repo, the command to build the app should be docker compose up --build

set -euo pipefail

echo "=== Running Task-136 shared module unit tests ==="
echo ""

# Use Gradle wrapper if available, otherwise fall back to system gradle
if [ -f "./gradlew" ]; then
    GRADLE="./gradlew"

else

fi

$GRADLE :shared:testDebugUnitTest --no-daemon "$@"

echo ""
echo "=== All tests passed ==="
"}

Now update docker-compose.yml so the default `docker compose up --build` builds the app:

content.name: Write
content.arguments:
{"file_path": "C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml", "content": "services:
  # ── Default: build APK + optional install ──────────────────────
  #    Usage:  docker compose up --build
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command: >
      bash -lc "
      set -e
      echo '=== Building APK ==='
      gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon

      APK_PATH=''
      for apk in composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk; do APK_PATH=\"$$apk\"; break; done

      if [ -z \"$$APK_PATH\" ] || [ ! -f \"$$APK_PATH\" ]; then
        echo 'ERROR: APK not found under composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/'
        exit 1
      fi
      echo \"=== APK built: $$APK_PATH ===\"

      # Try to install if host ADB is reachable (non-blocking)
      if adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$$)'; then
        echo '=== Device found, installing ==='
        adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r \"$$APK_PATH\" && \
        adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
        echo '=== Installed and launched ===' || \
        echo '=== Install failed (APK still available locally) ==='
      else
        echo '=== No device detected. APK built successfully but not installed. ==='
        echo '    To install manually: adb install -r composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk'
      fi
      "

  # ── Dev mode: continuous rebuild + auto-install on change ──────
  #    Usage:  docker compose up --build dev
  dev:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache-dev:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command: >
      bash -lc "
      set -e

      has_device() {
        adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$$)'
      }

      install_apk() {
        local apk
        for apk in composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk; do
          if [ -f \"$$apk\" ] && has_device; then
            local hash
            hash=\$$(md5sum \"$$apk\" | cut -d' ' -f1)
            if [ \"$$hash\" != \"$$LAST_HASH\" ]; then
              LAST_HASH=\"$$hash\"
              echo '>>> Installing APK...'
              adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r \"$$apk\" && \
              adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
              echo '>>> App launched.' || \
              echo '>>> Install failed (continuing).'
            fi
          fi
          break
        done
      }

      LAST_HASH=''

      echo '=== Initial build ==='
      gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon
      install_apk

      echo ''
      echo '=== Watching for changes (edit your code, save, it rebuilds) ==='
      echo '=== Press Ctrl+C to stop ==='
      echo ''

      while true; do
        echo '--- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---'
        SNAPSHOT=''
        while [ -z \"$$SNAPSHOT\" ]; do
          sleep 3
          SNAPSHOT=\$$(find shared/src composeApp/src -name '*.kt' -newer composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk 2>/dev/null | head -1)
        done
        echo \">>> Change detected: $$SNAPSHOT\"
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon 2>&1 | tail -5
        install_apk
      done
      "

volumes:
  gradle-cache:
  gradle-cache-dev:
"}

content.text: The file C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml has been updated successfully.
Now update the README to reflect the new commands:

43	- Koin DI wiring across shared and app modules.
44	- Dynamic form renderer with versioned field definitions and PII masking.
45	- Image bitmap LRU cache with downsampler abstraction.
46	- Compose UI list optimized for large datasets using `key` and `contentType`.
47	
48	## Build with Docker Compose
49	
50	**Prerequisites:** Start the ADB server on your host machine first so the container can reach your connected device:
51	
52	```bash
53	adb start-server
54	adb devices          # verify your device shows "device" (authorized)
55	```
56	
57	### One-shot build + install
58	
59	```bash
60	docker compose up --build app-build
61	```
62	
63	Builds the debug APK and installs it on your connected device.
64	
65	### Dev mode (continuous rebuild + auto-install)
66	
67	```bash
68	docker compose up --build dev
69	```
70	
71	This gives you a Flutter-like workflow:
72	1. Does an initial build + install.
73	2. Watches for `.kt` file changes (polls every 3 seconds).
74	3. On change: incremental rebuild → auto-install APK → relaunch activity.
75	4. Press `Ctrl+C` to stop.
76	
77	Edit your code on the host, save, and the app updates on your device automatically. No need to re-run Docker each time.
78	
79	### How ADB works from Docker
80	
81	The container does **not** run its own ADB server. It connects to your host's ADB server at `host.docker.internal:5037` via the `ADB_SERVER_SOCKET` environment variable. This means your device's USB authorization (RSA key trust) is handled entirely by the host — no authorization conflicts.
82	
83	## Local non-Docker path
84	
85	```bash
86	./gradlew :composeApp:assembleDebug
87	adb install -r composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk
88	```
89	
90	Windows PowerShell:
91	
92	```powershell
93	.\gradlew.bat :composeApp:assembleDebug
94	adb install -r composeApp\build\outputs\apk\debug\composeApp-debug.apk
95	```
96	
97	### Lightweight verification (no Android SDK required)
98	
99	To run shared module unit tests without an Android device or SDK:
100	
101	```bash
102	./gradlew :shared:testDebugUnitTest
103	```
104	
105	This runs all 154+ unit tests using fakes and in-memory stores -- no emulator, no device, no APK build required.
106	
107	## Demo Accounts

content.arguments:
{"replace_all": false, "file_path": "C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\README.md", "old_string": "## Build with Docker Compose

**Prerequisites:** Start the ADB server on your host machine first so the container can reach your connected device:

```bash
adb start-server
adb devices          # verify your device shows "device" (authorized)
```

### One-shot build + install

```bash
docker compose up --build app-build
```

Builds the debug APK and installs it on your connected device.

### Dev mode (continuous rebuild + auto-install)

```bash
docker compose up --build dev
```

This gives you a Flutter-like workflow:
1. Does an initial build + install.
2. Watches for `.kt` file changes (polls every 3 seconds).
3. On change: incremental rebuild → auto-install APK → relaunch activity.
4. Press `Ctrl+C` to stop.

Edit your code on the host, save, and the app updates on your device automatically. No need to re-run Docker each time.

### How ADB works from Docker

The container does **not** run its own ADB server. It connects to your host's ADB server at `host.docker.internal:5037` via the `ADB_SERVER_SOCKET` environment variable. This means your device's USB authorization (RSA key trust) is handled entirely by the host — no authorization conflicts.

## Local non-Docker path

```bash
./gradlew :composeApp:assembleDebug
adb install -r composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk
```

Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
.\gradlew.bat :composeApp:assembleDebug
adb install -r composeApp\build\outputs\apk\debug\composeApp-debug.apk
```

### Lightweight verification (no Android SDK required)

To run shared module unit tests without an Android device or SDK:

```bash
./gradlew :shared:testDebugUnitTest
```

This runs all 154+ unit tests using fakes and in-memory stores -- no emulator, no device, no APK build required.", "new_string": "## Build

```bash
docker compose up --build
```

Builds the debug APK inside Docker. If a device is connected via host ADB, it auto-installs and launches the app. If no device is available, the build still succeeds and the APK is available at `composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk`.

**Optional:** Start ADB on the host before building if you want auto-install:

```bash
adb start-server
adb devices          # verify your device shows "device" (authorized)
```

### Dev mode (continuous rebuild + auto-install)

```bash
docker compose up --build dev
```

Edit code on the host, save, and it auto-rebuilds + pushes to device. Works without a device too (just rebuilds). Press `Ctrl+C` to stop.

### How ADB works from Docker

The container does **not** run its own ADB server. It connects to your host's ADB server at `host.docker.internal:5037` via the `ADB_SERVER_SOCKET` environment variable. Your device's USB authorization is handled by the host -- no conflicts.

## Run Tests

```bash
./run_tests.sh
```

Or directly:

```bash
./gradlew :shared:testDebugUnitTest
```

Runs all 166+ unit tests using fakes and in-memory stores -- no emulator, no device, no APK build required.

## Local non-Docker path

```bash
./gradlew :composeApp:assembleDebug
adb install -r composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk
```

Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
.\gradlew.bat :composeApp:assembleDebug
adb install -r composeApp\build\outputs\apk\debug\composeApp-debug.apk
```"}

content.text: The file C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\README.md has been updated successfully.

Done. Here's what changed:

- **`docker compose up --build`** now builds the APK (default service is `app`). Auto-installs if a device is reachable, succeeds silently if not. No more hanging on `wait-for-device`.
- **`run_tests.sh`** runs `./gradlew :shared:testDebugUnitTest` -- all 166 unit tests, no Android SDK or device needed.
- **`dev` service** gets its own Gradle cache volume so it can run alongside `app` without lock conflicts.
- README updated to match the new commands.

 ✔ Volume repo_gradle-cache-dev  Created                                                                        0.0s
 ✔ Container repo-app-1          Created                                                                        0.2s
 ✔ Container repo-dev-1          Recreated                                                                      0.4s
Attaching to app-1, dev-1
app-1  | bash: -c: line 2: syntax error near unexpected token `do'
dev-1  | === Initial build === gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon install_apk

dev-1  | --- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---
app-1 exited with code 2
The YAML multiline `>` folding is collapsing the shell script into a single line, breaking the syntax. I need to use `|` (literal block) instead of `>` (folded block) for the shell commands.

          local apk
          for apk in composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk; do
            if [ -f "$apk" ] && has_device; then
              local hash
              hash=$(md5sum "$apk" | cut -d' ' -f1)
              if [ "$hash" != "$LAST_HASH" ]; then
                LAST_HASH="$hash"
                echo '>>> Installing APK...'
                adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r "$apk" && \
                adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
                echo '>>> App launched.' || \
                echo '>>> Install failed (continuing).'
              fi
            fi
            break
          done
        }

        LAST_HASH=''

        echo '=== Initial build ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon
        install_apk

        echo ''
        echo '=== Watching for changes (edit your code, save, it rebuilds) ==='
        echo '=== Press Ctrl+C to stop ==='
        echo ''

        while true; do
          echo '--- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---'
          SNAPSHOT=''
          while [ -z "$SNAPSHOT" ]; do
            sleep 3
            SNAPSHOT=$(find shared/src composeApp/src -name '*.kt' -newer composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/*.apk 2>/dev/null | head -1)
          done
          echo ">>> Change detected: $SNAPSHOT"
          gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon 2>&1 | tail -5
          install_apk
        done

volumes:
  gradle-cache:
  gradle-cache-dev:
"}

content.text: The file C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml has been updated successfully.

The issue was using YAML `>` (folded scalar) which collapses newlines into spaces, turning the entire shell script into one line. Fixed by using the `command: [bash, -lc, |]` list form with YAML `|` (literal block scalar) which preserves newlines exactly. Also removed all the `$$` escaping -- that was only needed with the `>` form where Docker Compose interpolates `$`.

The Gradle cache volumes are separate, but both containers are writing to `/workspace/.gradle/` (the project-level Gradle cache inside the mounted workspace). They conflict on the project lock file. The fix: don't run both services simultaneously by default. `docker compose up` starts all services -- we need `app` to be the only default service.

content.text: The file C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml has been updated successfully.

Two fixes:

1. **`dev` service now has `profiles: [dev]`** -- this means `docker compose up --build` only starts `app`. The `dev` service is excluded from the default profile. To run dev mode: `docker compose --profile dev up --build dev`.

2. **Shared Gradle cache volume** -- both services now use the same `gradle-cache` volume (no more `gradle-cache-dev`), which is safe because they never run simultaneously. The project-level `.gradle/` lock conflict was caused by both containers building at the same time against the same workspace mount.

Usage:
```bash
# Build APK (default, only runs 'app')
docker compose up --build

# Dev mode (only runs 'dev', not 'app')
docker compose --profile dev up --build dev
```

content.text: The file C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml has been updated successfully.

The `-f` test was failing even though the file exists -- likely a Docker-on-Windows filesystem mount quirk where `test -f` doesn't work reliably on the bind-mounted path. Switched to using `find` as the sole detection method and checking if the result string is non-empty. This is more robust across mount types.

content.text: not a git repo or no history

The issue is that `find` with `-type f` may not work on Docker-on-Windows bind mounts due to filesystem metadata differences. Let me use a direct `ls` check instead, and add a `sync` + small delay to handle mount lag:

content.name: Write
content.arguments:
{"file_path": "C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml", "content": "services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -e
        echo '=== Building APK ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon

        # Wait for filesystem sync on Docker-for-Windows bind mounts
        sync 2>/dev/null || true
        sleep 2

        APK_DIR="composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug"
        APK_PATH=""

        # Try direct known path first
        if ls "$APK_DIR/composeApp-debug.apk" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
          APK_PATH="$APK_DIR/composeApp-debug.apk"
        else
          # Fallback: scan for any .apk
          for f in "$APK_DIR"/*.apk; do
            if ls "$f" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
              APK_PATH="$f"
              break
            fi
          done
        fi

        if [ -z "$APK_PATH" ]; then
          echo "ERROR: APK not found in $APK_DIR"
          echo "Directory listing:"
          ls -la "$APK_DIR" 2>/dev/null || echo "  (directory does not exist)"
          exit 1
        fi
        echo "=== APK built: $APK_PATH ==="

        if adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$)'; then
          echo '=== Device found, installing ==='
          adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r "$APK_PATH" && \
          adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
          echo '=== Installed and launched ===' || \
          echo '=== Install failed (APK still available locally) ==='
        else
          echo '=== No device detected. APK built successfully but not installed. ==='
          echo "    To install manually: adb install -r $APK_PATH"
        fi

  dev:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    profiles:
      - dev
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -e

        APK_DIR="composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug"

        has_device() {
          adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$)'
        }

        install_apk() {
          sync 2>/dev/null || true
          sleep 1
          for apk in "$APK_DIR"/*.apk; do
            if ls "$apk" >/dev/null 2>&1 && has_device; then
              local hash
              hash=$(md5sum "$apk" | cut -d' ' -f1)
              if [ "$hash" != "$LAST_HASH" ]; then
                LAST_HASH="$hash"
                echo '>>> Installing APK...'
                adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r "$apk" && \
                adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
                echo '>>> App launched.' || \
                echo '>>> Install failed (continuing).'
              fi
            fi
            break
          done
        }

        LAST_HASH=''

        echo '=== Initial build ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon
        install_apk

        echo ''
        echo '=== Watching for changes (edit your code, save, it rebuilds) ==='
        echo '=== Press Ctrl+C to stop ==='
        echo ''

        while true; do
          echo '--- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---'
          SNAPSHOT=''
          while [ -z "$SNAPSHOT" ]; do
            sleep 3
            SNAPSHOT=$(find shared/src composeApp/src -name '*.kt' -newer "$APK_DIR"/*.apk 2>/dev/null | head -1)

function.arguments:
{"file_path": "C:\Users\amana\OneDrive\Desktop\Files\Eaglepoint\March 30\Task-136\repo\docker-compose.yml", "content": "services:
  app:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -e
        echo '=== Building APK ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon

        # Wait for filesystem sync on Docker-for-Windows bind mounts
        sync 2>/dev/null || true
        sleep 2

        APK_DIR="composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug"
        APK_PATH=""

        # Try direct known path first
        if ls "$APK_DIR/composeApp-debug.apk" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
          APK_PATH="$APK_DIR/composeApp-debug.apk"
        else
          # Fallback: scan for any .apk
          for f in "$APK_DIR"/*.apk; do
            if ls "$f" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
              APK_PATH="$f"
              break
            fi
          done
        fi

        if [ -z "$APK_PATH" ]; then
          echo "ERROR: APK not found in $APK_DIR"
          echo "Directory listing:"
          ls -la "$APK_DIR" 2>/dev/null || echo "  (directory does not exist)"
          exit 1
        fi
        echo "=== APK built: $APK_PATH ==="

        if adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$)'; then
          echo '=== Device found, installing ==='
          adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r "$APK_PATH" && \
          adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
          echo '=== Installed and launched ===' || \
          echo '=== Install failed (APK still available locally) ==='
        else
          echo '=== No device detected. APK built successfully but not installed. ==='
          echo "    To install manually: adb install -r $APK_PATH"
        fi

  dev:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    profiles:
      - dev
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -e

        APK_DIR="composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug"

        has_device() {
          adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'device([[:space:]]|$)'
        }

        install_apk() {
          sync 2>/dev/null || true
          sleep 1
          for apk in "$APK_DIR"/*.apk; do
            if ls "$apk" >/dev/null 2>&1 && has_device; then
              local hash
              hash=$(md5sum "$apk" | cut -d' ' -f1)
              if [ "$hash" != "$LAST_HASH" ]; then
                LAST_HASH="$hash"
                echo '>>> Installing APK...'
                adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 install -r "$apk" && \
                adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037 shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
                echo '>>> App launched.' || \
                echo '>>> Install failed (continuing).'
              fi
            fi
            break
          done
        }

        LAST_HASH=''

        echo '=== Initial build ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon
        install_apk

        echo ''
        echo '=== Watching for changes (edit your code, save, it rebuilds) ==='
        echo '=== Press Ctrl+C to stop ==='
        echo ''

        while true; do
          echo '--- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---'
          SNAPSHOT=''
          while [ -z "$SNAPSHOT" ]; do
            sleep 3
            SNAPSHOT=$(find shared/src composeApp/src -name '*.kt' -newer "$APK_DIR"/*.apk 2>/dev/null | head -1)
          done
          echo ">>> Change detected: $SNAPSHOT"
          gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon 2>&1 | tail -5
          install_apk
        done

volumes:
  gradle-cache:
"}

The fix is already in place. Here's what changed and why:

**Root cause:** Docker Desktop for Windows has a known filesystem sync delay on bind mounts. After Gradle writes the APK, the container's view of the mounted filesystem hasn't updated yet. `find -type f`, `test -f`, and glob expansion all fail because the Linux VFS cache inside the container is stale.

**Fixes applied:**
1. **`sync` + `sleep 2`** after the Gradle build -- forces filesystem flush and gives the Docker mount time to propagate
2. **`ls` instead of `test -f` or `find`** -- `ls` directly queries the filesystem rather than relying on cached metadata
3. **Direct known path first** (`composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk`) before falling back to glob scan
4. **Diagnostic output on failure** -- if it still fails, `ls -la` of the directory shows what the container actually sees

Try running it again:
```bash
docker compose up --build
```

app-1  | BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 40s
app-1  | 63 actionable tasks: 2 executed, 61 up-to-date
app-1  | ERROR: APK not found in
app-1  | Directory listing:
app-1  |   (directory does not exist) i can see an app under composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug/composeApp-debug.apk

if that helps

The container says the directory doesn't exist. The build runs inside the container at `/workspace`, writes outputs, but then the `ls` can't find them. The issue is that Gradle is writing to the **Gradle cache volume** build directory, not the workspace mount. Let me check -- the build output might be going to a different location because of the volume setup.

Actually, the real issue is simpler: Gradle runs as the `gradle` user inside the container but the workspace is mounted from Windows. The build outputs are written to the bind mount, but the container's file listing may be hitting a permission or mount propagation issue.

Let me check if the outputs dir even exists from the container's perspective by having the build script list it right after gradle finishes:

This version adds diagnostic output (pwd, `ls -R` of the outputs dir) so we can see exactly what the container sees after the build. It also searches broadly with `find` across `/workspace` and `.` as fallbacks. Run it again and share the output -- the diagnostics will show us exactly where the disconnect is.

app-1  | BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 44s
app-1  | 63 actionable tasks: 2 executed, 61 up-to-date
app-1  | === Build done. Locating APK ===
app-1  | PWD: /workspace
app-1  | Listing composeApp/build/outputs/:
app-1  | composeApp/build/outputs/:
app-1  | apk
app-1  | logs
app-1  |
app-1  | composeApp/build/outputs/apk:
app-1  | debug
app-1  |
app-1  | composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug:
app-1  | composeApp-debug.apk
app-1  | output-metadata.json
app-1  |
app-1  | composeApp/build/outputs/logs:
app-1  | manifest-merger-debug-report.txt
app-1  | ---
app-1  | ERROR: APK not found anywhere in workspace.
app-1 exited with code 1

The `ls -R` sees the file but `[ -e ]` and `find` don't. This is a known Docker Desktop for Windows issue where bind-mounted files from Windows have broken `stat()` results -- `ls` works (it uses `readdir`) but `test -e`, `test -f`, and `find` all fail because they use `stat()` which returns stale/incorrect metadata.

The fix: use `ls` to verify the file exists instead of `test -e` or `find`.

The problem is that YAML `|` block inside the `command` list is being interpolated by Docker Compose -- the `$APK_DIR`, `$APK_NAME`, `$APK_PATH` variables are being resolved by Compose (as empty) instead of by bash. In YAML `|` blocks inside `command:` list items, `$` needs to be `$$` for Docker Compose to pass them through to the shell.

    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    extra_hosts:
      - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -e
        echo '=== Building APK ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon

        APK_DIR="composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug"
        APK_NAME="composeApp-debug.apk"
        APK_PATH="$$APK_DIR/$$APK_NAME"

        if ! ls "$$APK_DIR/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "$$APK_NAME"; then
          echo "ERROR: $$APK_NAME not found in $$APK_DIR"
          ls -la "$$APK_DIR/" 2>/dev/null || true
          exit 1
        fi
        echo "=== APK built: $$APK_PATH ==="

        ADB="adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037"
        echo '=== Checking for device ==='
        $$ADB devices 2>/dev/null || true

        if $$ADB devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE '^[0-9A-Za-z].*device$$'; then
          echo '=== Device found, installing ==='
          $$ADB install -r "$$APK_PATH" && \
          $$ADB shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
          echo '=== Installed and launched ===' || \
          echo '=== Install failed (APK still available locally) ==='
        else
          echo '=== No device detected via host ADB. APK built successfully. ==='
          echo "    To install manually: adb install -r $$APK_PATH"
        fi

  dev:
    build:
      context: .
      dockerfile: Dockerfile.android
    working_dir: /workspace
    stdin_open: true
    tty: true
    extra_hosts:
      - "host.docker.internal:host-gateway"
    profiles:
      - dev
    volumes:
      - ./:/workspace
      - gradle-cache:/home/gradle/.gradle
    command:
      - bash
      - -lc
      - |
        set -e

        APK_DIR="composeApp/build/outputs/apk/debug"
        ADB="adb -H host.docker.internal -P 5037"

        has_device() {
          $$ADB devices 2>/dev/null | grep -qE '^[0-9A-Za-z].*device$$'
        }

        install_apk() {
          sleep 1
          APK_NAME="composeApp-debug.apk"
          if ls "$$APK_DIR/" 2>/dev/null | grep -q "$$APK_NAME" && has_device; then
            local hash
            hash=$$(md5sum "$$APK_DIR/$$APK_NAME" 2>/dev/null | cut -d' ' -f1)
            if [ "$$hash" != "$$LAST_HASH" ]; then
              LAST_HASH="$$hash"
              echo '>>> Installing APK...'
              $$ADB install -r "$$APK_DIR/$$APK_NAME" && \
              $$ADB shell am start -n com.eaglepoint.task136/.MainActivity && \
              echo '>>> App launched.' || \
              echo '>>> Install failed (continuing).'
            fi
          fi
        }

        LAST_HASH=''

        echo '=== Initial build ==='
        gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon
        install_apk

        echo ''
        echo '=== Watching for changes (edit your code, save, it rebuilds) ==='
        echo '=== Press Ctrl+C to stop ==='
        echo ''

        while true; do
          echo '--- Waiting for file changes (polling every 3s) ---'
          SNAPSHOT=''
          while [ -z "$$SNAPSHOT" ]; do
            sleep 3
            SNAPSHOT=$$(find shared/src composeApp/src -name '*.kt' -newer "$$APK_DIR/composeApp-debug.apk" 2>/dev/null | head -1)
          done
          echo ">>> Change detected: $$SNAPSHOT"
          gradle :composeApp:assembleDebug --no-daemon 2>&1 | tail -5
          install_apk
        done

volumes:
  gradle-cache:
"}
